EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of Saturday, August 13th, a warm front lifted northward across the region. This allowed a very warm and excessively humid air mass to be in place over the area. ||Late in the day, a surface cold front was approaching the area from the west. An organized line of thunderstorms moved from west to east across all of eastern Upstate New York and crossed western Massachusetts during the evening hours. These storms produced gusty winds, which caused damage to some trees and power lines. ||In addition to the gusty winds, the thunderstorms also produced a significant amount of cloud-to-ground lightning. Numerous power outages were reported across the region as a result of the thunderstorms. ||Thunderstorms ended with the passage of the cold front during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in Pittsfield due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation was below normal for most of Massachusetts during December, but with areas of above normal amounts along the North Shore and around Quabbin Reservoir in Central Massachusetts.  Amounts in Southeast Massachusetts were near 2 inches below normal while Amherst was near 1 inch above normal.||A Drought Warning remains in effect for most of Massachusetts. A Drought Advisory continued for Cape Cod and the Islands. ||Although rivers and streams had some fluctuations during the month, they were predominantly at below normal levels during December. Reservoirs monitored by Massachusetts Department of Conservation and Recreation (DCR), were mainly at below normal levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor continued the Severe Drought (D2) designation in Suffolk County through the month of December.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of snow fell on southern Worcester County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified as it moved off the coast of North Carolina and tracked northeastward, passing south of southern New England.  This brought accumulating snow to areas south of Interstate 90 in Massachusetts, including Connecticut and Rhode Island.  In addition, strong, damaging winds accompanied the snow.  With bare trees, there was remarkably little damage associated with winds that gusted near hurricane force at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to seven inches of snow fell across interior Plymouth County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind an Arctic cold front, very cold temperatures poured into the region upon brisk northwesterly winds. Temperatures dropped through the day on Saturday, February 13th, reaching lows of 10 to 25 degrees below zero across western Massachusetts on Saturday night. Along with winds gusting 20 to 40 MPH, wind chill values reached 25 to 45 degrees below zero for Saturday night into Sunday morning. ||With temperatures warming up and winds diminishing, the threat for dangerously cold temperatures and low wind chills ended on the afternoon hours of Sunday, February 14th.

Temperatures dropped through the day on Saturday, February 13th, reaching lows of 10 to 25 degrees below zero across western Massachusetts on Saturday night. Along with winds gusting 20 to 40 MPH, wind chill values reached 25 to 45 degrees below zero for Saturday night into Sunday morning. ||With temperatures warming up and winds diminishing, the threat for dangerously cold temperatures and low wind chills ended on the afternoon hours of Sunday, February 14th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed in Leverett, blocking Dudleyville Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure in the Gulf of Maine combined with approaching high pressure to develop a very strong pressure gradient over southern New England. This resulted in strong, sometimes damaging winds across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system over the Canadian Maritimes and strong high pressure over the Great Lakes resulted in a strong pressure gradient over southern New England.  This produced strong, gusty winds that produced some minor wind damage in parts of Massachusetts and Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto Huron Avenue in Cambridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified as it moved off the coast of North Carolina and tracked northeastward, passing south of southern New England.  This brought accumulating snow to areas south of Interstate 90 in Massachusetts, including Connecticut and Rhode Island.  In addition, strong, damaging winds accompanied the snow.  With bare trees, there was remarkably little damage associated with winds that gusted near hurricane force at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to seven inches of snow fell across northern Bristol County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed south of New England Friday night the 16th, moved along the Southern New England coast on Saturday the 17th, and moved to the Maritimes by Sunday the 18th.  This combined with cold air already in place over the region brought a broad area of snow over Southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inches of snow fell on northern Bristol County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked north through New York bringing a warm front through southern New England. Unseasonably deep moisture accompanied this front. This was a very complicated weather situation as a strong low level inversion was in place over the area with a very strong low level jet just above the inversion. As showers and thunderstorms developed, the storms and heavy rain allowed the stronger winds to mix down to the surface. In other areas, temperatures warmed enough at the surface to break the inversion and allow the stronger winds to mix down. This resulted in a complicated combination of severe thunderstorm winds and high winds. To add to the historical nature of this event, it occurred in February all during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto a car at the intersection of Partridgeville and Coolidge Roads in Athol.

A strong low level jet combined with the heavy rain to result in intermittently strong winds mixing to the surface.  These winds resulted in scattered tree and power line damage across southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed on McClellan Highway in Boston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very powerful low pressure system tracked up the east coast, passing southeast of Southern New England. This storm brought heavy snow and gusty winds, resulting in blizzard conditions along the Massachusetts east coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to eleven inches of snow fell across Cape Cod.  Blizzard conditions were observed at three locations on Cape Cod.  They occurred at Falmouth (KFMH) from 1115am to 315pm, Hyannis (KHYA) from 917am to 156pm, and Chatham (CQX) from 914am to 211pm, all on February 8th.  Snow began prior to blizzard conditions being observed and continued long after they ceased.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked north through New York bringing a warm front through southern New England. Unseasonably deep moisture accompanied this front. This was a very complicated weather situation as a strong low level inversion was in place over the area with a very strong low level jet just above the inversion. As showers and thunderstorms developed, the storms and heavy rain allowed the stronger winds to mix down to the surface. In other areas, temperatures warmed enough at the surface to break the inversion and allow the stronger winds to mix down. This resulted in a complicated combination of severe thunderstorm winds and high winds. To add to the historical nature of this event, it occurred in February all during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires on Smith and Slater Streets in Attleboro was downed by high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure strengthened rapidly as it tracked from the Gulf Coast to upstate New York and Quebec. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified as it moved off the coast of North Carolina and tracked northeastward, passing south of southern New England.  This brought accumulating snow to areas south of Interstate 90 in Massachusetts, including Connecticut and Rhode Island.  In addition, strong, damaging winds accompanied the snow.  With bare trees, there was remarkably little damage associated with winds that gusted near hurricane force at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving clipper storm moved from the Great Lakes towards the Northeast on Monday, April 4th. With an unseasonably chilly air mass in place, precipitation fell in the form of snow across the entire region. Although the snow was generally light in intensity, there were a few moderate bursts of snow, especially during the early morning hours. ||Although the snow accumulated best on grassy surfaces, roads become slippery and the morning commute was difficult in many locations. Although some schools cancelled classes, many remained open and there was a large number of auto accidents and traffic on roadways.||By the time snow tapered off in the late afternoon or early evening hours, 5 to 7 inches of snow accumulated across the area.

Low pressure tracked north through New York bringing a warm front through southern New England. Unseasonably deep moisture accompanied this front. This was a very complicated weather situation as a strong low level inversion was in place over the area with a very strong low level jet just above the inversion. As showers and thunderstorms developed, the storms and heavy rain allowed the stronger winds to mix down to the surface. In other areas, temperatures warmed enough at the surface to break the inversion and allow the stronger winds to mix down. This resulted in a complicated combination of severe thunderstorm winds and high winds. To add to the historical nature of this event, it occurred in February all during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires on Chartley Brook Lane were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful area of low pressure was located well off the eastern seaboard on the morning of Monday, February 8th. As this storm lifted northeast, the steadiest and heaviest snowfall from this system impacted much of eastern New England. However, some of the snowfall extended back into parts of western New England for the afternoon and evening hours. Three to 7 inches of snow occurred across Berkshire County, before snowfall tapered off by the late evening hours.  The heaviest amounts fell across the higher elevations of the Berkshires in eastern parts of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure traveling along a cold front stalled south of southern New England brought heavy rain, which changed over to heavy snow as temperatures dropped. This snow was extraordinarily wet and heavy, bringing down trees and wires across portions of southern New England.  Power outages reached a peak of approximately 107,000 customers without power in Massachusetts during the peak of the storm, mainly across eastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to ten inches of snow fell across interior Essex County.  In addition, heavy snow downed wires on Uptack Road and Main Street in Groveland.  In North Andover, wires were downed on Marble Ridge Road.  A tree was downed onto a house on Prospect Street in Lawrence.  A large tree fell on a truck blocking North Main Street in Andover.  In Boxford, wires were downed onto Topsfield Road.  A large tree was downed onto Route 125 in Andover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked north through New York bringing a warm front through southern New England. Unseasonably deep moisture accompanied this front. This was a very complicated weather situation as a strong low level inversion was in place over the area with a very strong low level jet just above the inversion. As showers and thunderstorms developed, the storms and heavy rain allowed the stronger winds to mix down to the surface. In other areas, temperatures warmed enough at the surface to break the inversion and allow the stronger winds to mix down. This resulted in a complicated combination of severe thunderstorm winds and high winds. To add to the historical nature of this event, it occurred in February all during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple wires were downed throughout North Attleboro.  A tree and wires on Wilmarth Street in Attleboro were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure was located over the Ohio Valley on Wednesday, February 24th and the storm headed northeast towards the region. A warm front extended out of this storm, allowing for periods of rainfall during the day Wednesday, February 24th and into the evening hours. As the warm front passed through the region during the overnight hours, temperatures became quite mild for late February reaching into the 50s and 60s, despite it being in the middle of the night. In addition, thunderstorms ahead of the storm's cold front moved from the mid-Atlantic states up towards western Massachusetts.||The thunderstorms produced a period of very heavy rainfall during the late evening and into the overnight hours. Rainfall fell at rates exceeding one inch per hour at times. This rainfall, combined with a frozen ground in places and some snow melt in mountainous areas, allowed for widespread flooding of urban, poor drainage and low lying areas. ||In addition, the narrow line of thunderstorms became severe over the northern Berkshires, producing damaging wind gusts. The strongest gusts occurred in the Williamstown area, downing several trees and damaging some homes.||The passage of the storm's cold front during the early morning hours of Thursday, February 25th ended the threat for severe thunderstorms and heavy rainfall. Some additional rainfall during the day on Thursday, February 25th allowed the receding of water of flooded areas to be slow to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fence was damaged due to thunderstorm wind gusts along Galvin Road in Williamstown and pieces of it were thrown through the 2nd floor window of a home.  In addition, several trees were damaged along Galvin Road and some of them fell on homes, damaging the roofs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance coupled with cold air aloft and moving into southern New England set off a complicated mix of showers and thunderstorms and non-convective winds.  All efforts were made to separate out the non-convective winds from the thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree on Prospect Avenue in Quincy was downed onto a house and a car, resulting in damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance coupled with cold air aloft and moving into southern New England set off a complicated mix of showers and thunderstorms and non-convective winds.  All efforts were made to separate out the non-convective winds from the thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed in Norton.  A tree on Tremont Street in Taunton was downed by strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very powerful low pressure system tracked up the east coast, passing southeast of Southern New England. This storm brought heavy snow and gusty winds, resulting in blizzard conditions along the Massachusetts east coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven to ten inches of snow fell across Martha's Vineyard.  Blizzard conditions occurred at Vineyard Haven (KMVY) from 903am to 145pm on February 8th.  Snow began prior to blizzard conditions being observed and continued after they ceased.  The Automated Surface Observing System at Vineyard Haven (KMVY) recorded sustained wind speeds of 40 mph at 412am.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to eight inches of snow fell across southern Worcester County.  The combination of winds and snow brought down a tree on Chester Street in Worcester.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of snow fell across western Norfolk County.  The combination of winds and snow brought down a tree onto a car on Orchard Street in Millis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very powerful low pressure system tracked up the east coast, passing southeast of Southern New England. This storm brought heavy snow and gusty winds, resulting in blizzard conditions along the Massachusetts east coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to eight inches of snow fell across eastern Plymouth County.  Blizzard conditions occurred at Plymouth (KPYM) from 925am to 109pm.  Snow began prior to blizzard conditions being observed and continued after they ceased.  An amateur radio operator recorded a 61 mph wind gust on their home weather station in Scituate.  The combination of winds and snow brought down a tree and wires in Plymouth and wires on Rock Road in Scituate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very powerful low pressure system tracked up the east coast, passing southeast of Southern New England. This storm brought heavy snow and gusty winds, resulting in blizzard conditions along the Massachusetts east coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to six inches of snow fell across Nantucket.  Blizzard conditions occurred at Nantucket (KACK) from 920am to 223pm.  Snow began prior to blizzard conditions being observed and continued after they ceased.  The Automated Surface Observing System at Nantucket Memorial Airport (KACK) recorded sustained winds of 46 mph and a wind gust of 58 mph.  An amateur radio operator recorded a 65 mph wind gust at their home in the Quidnet section of Nantucket.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked north through New York bringing a warm front through southern New England. Unseasonably deep moisture accompanied this front. This was a very complicated weather situation as a strong low level inversion was in place over the area with a very strong low level jet just above the inversion. As showers and thunderstorms developed, the storms and heavy rain allowed the stronger winds to mix down to the surface. In other areas, temperatures warmed enough at the surface to break the inversion and allow the stronger winds to mix down. This resulted in a complicated combination of severe thunderstorm winds and high winds. To add to the historical nature of this event, it occurred in February all during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires in Middleborough were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with an upper level disturbance to produce strong to damaging winds across portions of southern New England.  This occurred mainly in the early morning hours shortly after midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Interstate 91 in Greenfield between exits 26 and 27.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Nantucket Memorial Airport (KACK) recorded sustained wind speeds of 41 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying clipper low pressure system passed north of southern New England, bringing an arctic cold front through the area.  This resulted in snow across much of the region and strong, damaging winds near the coast.  Unseasonably cold weather then spread across southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 49 year old man and a 51 year old woman were killed when strong winds combined with snow resulted in a severely rotted tree being blown over onto their car.  Winds from stations in the area were generally gusting up to about 50 mph, with sustained winds speeds in the 30 to 40 mph range.  This occurred on Rockland Street in Abington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Matthew was a major hurricane in the Caribbean and Bahamas, then weakened along the Southeast US coast and became post-tropical off North Carolina. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid air mass was in place over western Massachusetts during the day on Sunday, May 29th. Although strong upper level ridging was initially in place over the area, an upper level trough started to approach from the west, with a surface cold front moving towards the area from Canada. ||During the afternoon hours, bands of slow moving showers and thunderstorms broke out over the Berkshires and slowly moved northeast. Due to the slow movement of these thunderstorms, heavy rainfall repeatedly fell over the same area in the Lee and Stockbridge portions of Berkshire County.  This resulted in flash flooding occurring across the area, as some roads were temporarily closed due to being covered by water and debris.   The flooding was so significant in Stockbridge that town officials declared a state of emergency. Spotters measured up to five inches of rainfall falling in just a few hours. ||In addition, the thunderstorms produced gusty winds.  This downed some trees and power lines across the area as well.||With the loss of daytime heating, showers and thunderstorms ended by the evening hours and the weather was quiet into the overnight hours. This allowed water to quickly recede across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from slow moving thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding in Lee.  East Center Street was impassable due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brush fire on Tekoa Mountain near Westfield, Massachusetts started on March 8, 2016 and spread quickly thanks to very dry weather in the previous days and weeks.  Weather on March 8th and 9th was favorable for fire fighters with light winds and relative humidity values around 50%.  Despite this, the fire spread to nearly 70 acres before fire fighters were able to contain it.  One of the reasons for the quick fire spread was the lack of hydrants or water lines in the vicinity, requiring fire fighters to carry in water on their backs.  By 2pm on March 9, fire fighters deemed the fire 90 percent contained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brush fire on Tekoa Mountain near Westfield, Massachusetts started on March 8, 2016 and spread quickly thanks to very dry weather in the previous days and weeks. Weather on March 8th and 9th was favorable for fire fighters with light winds and relative humidity values around 50%. Despite this, the fire spread to about 60 acres before fire fighters were able to contain it. One of the reasons for the quick fire spread was the lack of hydrants or water lines in the vicinity, requiring fire fighters to carry in water on their backs. By 2pm on March 9, fire fighters deemed the fire 90 percent contained.  The area of the fire was very remote and no structures were in the area of the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mild and humid air mass was in place over western Massachusetts during the early morning hours on Sunday, September 11th. A cold front was crossing through the region around sunrise and a potent line of thunderstorms accompanied the passage of this boundary. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hermine was named on August 31st as it intensified to a tropical storm in the Gulf of Mexico. She meandered about in the eastern Gulf of Mexico before moving northeastward across the Florida panhandle, through Georgia and then along the coast of North and South Carolina. She was a hurricane as she made landfall in Florida, then weakened to a tropical storm and finally a tropical depression as she moved off the coast of North Carolina. After moving off the coast of North Carolina, Hermine moved northeast, then north, then west, meandering south of southern New England for several days in early September. ||While classified as a tropical system, Hermine's effects on southern New England were rather minimal. Rainfall added up to less than an inch for most locations and winds were generally below tropical storm force. Because trees were still fully leaved and we had higher wind gusts, there was some wind damage. Storm surge was highest during low tide so we had minimal coastal flooding impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Nantucket Memorial Airport (KACK) recorded sustained wind speeds of 34 knots and gusts to 49 knots.  Multiple small boats sunk or were dragging anchor in Nantucket Harbor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: September brought more dry weather and continued drought conditions to much of southern New England. For many, the drought began in July 2016. At this point, this is a drought categorized largely by well below normal precipitation and groundwater and agricultural impacts. Most of the region experienced below normal rainfall during the month of September. ||The Massachusetts Executive Office of Energy and Environmental Affairs expanded the drought warning into the southeastern portion of the state, and a drought watch was expanded into Cape Cod and the Islands on September 9th. Soil moisture (measured by the Climate Prediction Center) was abnormally dry or drier. Groundwater conditions (measured by the United States Geological Survey) were found to be below to well below normal for most of Massachusetts. Cape Cod and the Islands were normal at this time. River and streamflow conditions were below to well below normal with 16 gage sites in southern New England at record low levels. ||Farmers have had to irrigate their crops much more than normal for September. Losses were started to be reported by producers in the Connecticut River valley area of Massachusetts. In addition, sources of irrigation have been dwindling. While data thus far has been anecdotal, many farmers are having to choose which fields to irrigate and which to let go, as well as not doing 2nd and 3rd plantings of late summer crops. Known losses, according to the Massachusetts Farm Bureau Federation, include a total loss of the peach crop, reduced apple and cranberry crops, and no second and third cut hay. The Massachusetts state government declared on September 7th to make up to $1 million in microloan funds available to small, family owned farms who suffered losses due to the drought.  Eleven counties in Massachusetts were declared primary disaster counties due to the drought.  They include: Barnstable, Berkshire, Bristol, Essex, Franklin, Hampden, Hampshire, Middlesex, Norfolk, Plymouth, and Worcester Counties.  Farms in these counties are eligible for assistance from the Farm Service Agency and the Livestock Forage Disaster Program.||More than 160 public water systems in Massachusetts have imposed some sort of water use restrictions, 70 of those have the strictest parameters in place, allowing residents to use outdoor water one day a week or less.  One of these was Worcester, the second largest city in Massachusetts.  Worcester banned outdoor water use in early September and ordered restaurants to stop serving tap water unless requested by a customer because their reservoir system had dropped to 55 percent of capacity.  It's reservoirs are typically at 80 percent of capacity going into the fall.  In addition, Worcester officials activated an emergency connection to draw water from the Massachusetts Water Resources Authority (MWRA) at a cost of $1.7 million per month.  Other communities that already rely on the MWRA for a portion of their water have increased their demand by 38 percent this year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor continued the Severe Drought (D2) designation in eastern Franklin County through the month of September.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires on Jenkins Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was above normal in October for Eastern Massachusetts and the Central Hills, but remained below normal in Western Massachusetts.  Values since July 2016 for all areas remained below normal. ||On October 7th, the Massachusetts Executive Office of Energy and Environmental Affairs expanded the Drought Warning to include the Connecticut River region. A Drought Warning remained in effect for the northeast, central, and southeast parts of the state. A Drought Watch continued for Cape Cod and parts of the Islands.  The Massachusetts Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) indicated mandatory non-essential outdoor water use restrictions were in place for numerous communities within central and eastern MA. Other communities in the Commonwealth had voluntary water restrictions in place.||Many gages in central and eastern portions of southern New England had at least somewhat improved streamflows. However, across the Connecticut River Valley in MA and CT, rivers and streams remained quite low.||At the end of October, many USGS groundwater wells across southern New England were at below to well below normal levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor placed southern Plymouth County in an Extreme Drought (D3) designation at the beginning of October. Then from October 11 through the end of the month, southern Plymouth County was in a Severe Drought (D2) designation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hermine was named on August 31st as it intensified to a tropical storm in the Gulf of Mexico. She meandered about in the eastern Gulf of Mexico before moving northeastward across the Florida panhandle, through Georgia and then along the coast of North and South Carolina. She was a hurricane as she made landfall in Florida, then weakened to a tropical storm and finally a tropical depression as she moved off the coast of North Carolina. After moving off the coast of North Carolina, Hermine moved northeast, then north, then west, meandering south of southern New England for several days in early September. ||While classified as a tropical system, Hermine's effects on southern New England were rather minimal. Rainfall added up to less than an inch for most locations and winds were generally below tropical storm force. Because trees were still fully leaved and we had higher wind gusts, there was some wind damage. Storm surge was highest during low tide so we had minimal coastal flooding impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WeatherFlow sites at Wellfleet and Sagamore Beach recorded sustained wind speeds of 28 and 31 knots, respectively.  The WeatherFlow site at Kalmus recorded wind gusts of 40 knots.  In Chatham, a large branch was downed at the intersection of Barn Hill Road and Aunt Nabby's Lane.  A tree was downed on Lakeview Avenue. A tree on Crow's Pond Road was downed.  In Falmouth, a large branch was downed onto power lines.  A tree was downed onto Martin Road.  In Brewster, a tree was downed onto a house on Gould Drive.  In East Sandwich, trees and limbs were downed onto Spring Hill Road.  In Sandwich, trees and wires were downed on Shore Drive.  In Eastham, wires were downed onto Cable Road.  In Yarmouth, a tree was downed onto Union Street.  In Harwich, a tree was downed onto Cedardale Road.  In Bourne, a tree was downed onto Sandwich Road near the mid-Cape connector.  In Mashpee, a large tree was downed, blocking Grenadier Lane.  In Wellfleet, Route 6 was closed in both directions between Cove Road and Nauhaught Bluff Road due to downed power lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and wires were downed in Cohasset.  A tree was downed onto a garage in Weymouth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: September brought more dry weather and continued drought conditions to much of southern New England. For many, the drought began in July 2016. At this point, this is a drought categorized largely by well below normal precipitation and groundwater and agricultural impacts. Most of the region experienced below normal rainfall during the month of September. ||The Massachusetts Executive Office of Energy and Environmental Affairs expanded the drought warning into the southeastern portion of the state, and a drought watch was expanded into Cape Cod and the Islands on September 9th. Soil moisture (measured by the Climate Prediction Center) was abnormally dry or drier. Groundwater conditions (measured by the United States Geological Survey) were found to be below to well below normal for most of Massachusetts. Cape Cod and the Islands were normal at this time. River and streamflow conditions were below to well below normal with 16 gage sites in southern New England at record low levels. ||Farmers have had to irrigate their crops much more than normal for September. Losses were started to be reported by producers in the Connecticut River valley area of Massachusetts. In addition, sources of irrigation have been dwindling. While data thus far has been anecdotal, many farmers are having to choose which fields to irrigate and which to let go, as well as not doing 2nd and 3rd plantings of late summer crops. Known losses, according to the Massachusetts Farm Bureau Federation, include a total loss of the peach crop, reduced apple and cranberry crops, and no second and third cut hay. The Massachusetts state government declared on September 7th to make up to $1 million in microloan funds available to small, family owned farms who suffered losses due to the drought.  Eleven counties in Massachusetts were declared primary disaster counties due to the drought.  They include: Barnstable, Berkshire, Bristol, Essex, Franklin, Hampden, Hampshire, Middlesex, Norfolk, Plymouth, and Worcester Counties.  Farms in these counties are eligible for assistance from the Farm Service Agency and the Livestock Forage Disaster Program.||More than 160 public water systems in Massachusetts have imposed some sort of water use restrictions, 70 of those have the strictest parameters in place, allowing residents to use outdoor water one day a week or less.  One of these was Worcester, the second largest city in Massachusetts.  Worcester banned outdoor water use in early September and ordered restaurants to stop serving tap water unless requested by a customer because their reservoir system had dropped to 55 percent of capacity.  It's reservoirs are typically at 80 percent of capacity going into the fall.  In addition, Worcester officials activated an emergency connection to draw water from the Massachusetts Water Resources Authority (MWRA) at a cost of $1.7 million per month.  Other communities that already rely on the MWRA for a portion of their water have increased their demand by 38 percent this year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor continued the Extreme Drought (D3) designation in western Middlesex County through the month of September.

A Drought Warning remained in effect for the northeast, central, and southeast parts of the state. A Drought Watch continued for Cape Cod and parts of the Islands.  The Massachusetts Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) indicated mandatory non-essential outdoor water use restrictions were in place for numerous communities within central and eastern MA. Other communities in the Commonwealth had voluntary water restrictions in place.||Many gages in central and eastern portions of southern New England had at least somewhat improved streamflows. However, across the Connecticut River Valley in MA and CT, rivers and streams remained quite low.||At the end of October, many USGS groundwater wells across southern New England were at below to well below normal levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor continued a Severe Drought (D2) designation for Dukes County through October 11. The designation was then reduced to Abnormally Dry (D0) through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: September brought more dry weather and continued drought conditions to much of southern New England. For many, the drought began in July 2016. At this point, this is a drought categorized largely by well below normal precipitation and groundwater and agricultural impacts. Most of the region experienced below normal rainfall during the month of September. ||The Massachusetts Executive Office of Energy and Environmental Affairs expanded the drought warning into the southeastern portion of the state, and a drought watch was expanded into Cape Cod and the Islands on September 9th. Soil moisture (measured by the Climate Prediction Center) was abnormally dry or drier. Groundwater conditions (measured by the United States Geological Survey) were found to be below to well below normal for most of Massachusetts. Cape Cod and the Islands were normal at this time. River and streamflow conditions were below to well below normal with 16 gage sites in southern New England at record low levels. ||Farmers have had to irrigate their crops much more than normal for September. Losses were started to be reported by producers in the Connecticut River valley area of Massachusetts. In addition, sources of irrigation have been dwindling. While data thus far has been anecdotal, many farmers are having to choose which fields to irrigate and which to let go, as well as not doing 2nd and 3rd plantings of late summer crops. Known losses, according to the Massachusetts Farm Bureau Federation, include a total loss of the peach crop, reduced apple and cranberry crops, and no second and third cut hay. The Massachusetts state government declared on September 7th to make up to $1 million in microloan funds available to small, family owned farms who suffered losses due to the drought.  Eleven counties in Massachusetts were declared primary disaster counties due to the drought.  They include: Barnstable, Berkshire, Bristol, Essex, Franklin, Hampden, Hampshire, Middlesex, Norfolk, Plymouth, and Worcester Counties.  Farms in these counties are eligible for assistance from the Farm Service Agency and the Livestock Forage Disaster Program.||More than 160 public water systems in Massachusetts have imposed some sort of water use restrictions, 70 of those have the strictest parameters in place, allowing residents to use outdoor water one day a week or less.  One of these was Worcester, the second largest city in Massachusetts.  Worcester banned outdoor water use in early September and ordered restaurants to stop serving tap water unless requested by a customer because their reservoir system had dropped to 55 percent of capacity.  It's reservoirs are typically at 80 percent of capacity going into the fall.  In addition, Worcester officials activated an emergency connection to draw water from the Massachusetts Water Resources Authority (MWRA) at a cost of $1.7 million per month.  Other communities that already rely on the MWRA for a portion of their water have increased their demand by 38 percent this year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor continued the Extreme Drought (D3) designation in southeastern Middlesex County through the month of September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mild and humid air mass was in place over western Massachusetts during the early morning hours on Sunday, September 11th. A cold front was crossing through the region around sunrise and a potent line of thunderstorms accompanied the passage of this boundary. Some of these storms were severe, producing damaging winds to trees and power lines. The thunderstorms didn't last long, but produced a swath of damage across Berkshire County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down on wires near North Otis due to thunderstorm winds.  The tree caught on fire as a result of the downed wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: September brought more dry weather and continued drought conditions to much of southern New England. For many, the drought began in July 2016. At this point, this is a drought categorized largely by well below normal precipitation and groundwater and agricultural impacts. Most of the region experienced below normal rainfall during the month of September. ||The Massachusetts Executive Office of Energy and Environmental Affairs expanded the drought warning into the southeastern portion of the state, and a drought watch was expanded into Cape Cod and the Islands on September 9th. Soil moisture (measured by the Climate Prediction Center) was abnormally dry or drier. Groundwater conditions (measured by the United States Geological Survey) were found to be below to well below normal for most of Massachusetts. Cape Cod and the Islands were normal at this time. River and streamflow conditions were below to well below normal with 16 gage sites in southern New England at record low levels. ||Farmers have had to irrigate their crops much more than normal for September. Losses were started to be reported by producers in the Connecticut River valley area of Massachusetts. In addition, sources of irrigation have been dwindling. While data thus far has been anecdotal, many farmers are having to choose which fields to irrigate and which to let go, as well as not doing 2nd and 3rd plantings of late summer crops. Known losses, according to the Massachusetts Farm Bureau Federation, include a total loss of the peach crop, reduced apple and cranberry crops, and no second and third cut hay. The Massachusetts state government declared on September 7th to make up to $1 million in microloan funds available to small, family owned farms who suffered losses due to the drought. Eleven counties in Massachusetts were declared primary disaster counties due to the drought. They include: Barnstable, Berkshire, Bristol, Essex, Franklin, Hampden, Hampshire, Middlesex, Norfolk, Plymouth, and Worcester Counties. Farms in these counties are eligible for assistance from the Farm Service Agency and the Livestock Forage Disaster Program. ||More than 160 public water systems in Massachusetts have imposed some sort of water use restrictions, 70 of those have the strictest parameters in place, allowing residents to use outdoor water one day a week or less. One of these was Worcester, the second largest city in Massachusetts. Worcester banned outdoor water use in early September and ordered restaurants to stop serving tap water unless requested by a customer because their reservoir system had dropped to 55 percent of capacity. It's reservoirs are typically at 80 percent of capacity going into the fall. In addition, Worcester officials activated an emergency connection to draw water from the Massachusetts Water Resources Authority (MWRA) at a cost of $1.7 million per month. Other communities that already rely on the MWRA for a portion of their water have increased their demand by 38 percent this year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor expanded the Severe Drought (D2) designation into Dukes County September 13th.  This designation continued through the remainder of September.

The Massachusetts Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) indicated mandatory non-essential outdoor water use restrictions were in place for numerous communities within central and eastern MA. Other communities in the Commonwealth had voluntary water restrictions in place.||Many gages in central and eastern portions of southern New England had at least somewhat improved streamflows. However, across the Connecticut River Valley in MA and CT, rivers and streams remained quite low.||At the end of October, many USGS groundwater wells across southern New England were at below to well below normal levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hermine was named on August 31st as it intensified to a tropical storm in the Gulf of Mexico. She meandered about in the eastern Gulf of Mexico before moving northeastward across the Florida panhandle, through Georgia and then along the coast of North and South Carolina. She was a hurricane as she made landfall in Florida, then weakened to a tropical storm and finally a tropical depression as she moved off the coast of North Carolina. After moving off the coast of North Carolina, Hermine moved northeast, then north, then west, meandering south of southern New England for several days in early September. ||While classified as a tropical system, Hermine's effects on southern New England were rather minimal. Rainfall added up to less than an inch for most locations and winds were generally below tropical storm force. Because trees were still fully leaved and we had higher wind gusts, there was some wind damage. Storm surge was highest during low tide so we had minimal coastal flooding impacts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: August brought more dry weather and continued drought conditions to much of southern New England. By the end of the month, most, if not all of Massachusetts was in a drought designation. For many, the drought began in July 2016. At this point, this is a drought categorized largely by well below normal precipitation and groundwater and agricultural impacts. Most of the region experienced below normal rainfall during the month of August. Much of eastern Massachusetts south of Boston, received less than half the normal amount of rainfall for the month. The exception was southern Worcester County where above normal rainfall was experienced. However, this additional rainfall did little to dispel drought conditions. Boston experienced its driest meteorological summer (June/July/August) on record with only 3.92 inches of rainfall. The previous record was 3.97 inches in 1957. ||The Massachusetts Executive Office of Energy and Environmental Affairs issued a drought warning for the northeast and central portions of the state, a drought watch for southeast Massachusetts and the Connecticut River Valley of Massachusetts, and a drought advisory for western Massachusetts and Cape Cod and the Islands on August 12th. This was an upgrade for each region from July. Of note: this is the first time a Drought Warning has been in effect for any portion of Massachusetts since the original creation of the state's drought plan in 2001. Soil moisture (measured by the Climate Prediction Center) was abnormally dry, except across east coastal Massachusetts where conditions were found to be excessively dry. Groundwater conditions (measured by the United States Geological Survey) were found to be below to well below normal for most of Massachusetts. Cape Cod and the Islands were at or above normal at this time. River and streamflow conditions were below to well below normal with 27 gage sites in southern New England at record low levels. ||Farmers have had to irrigate their crops much more than normal for August. Losses were started to be reported by producers in the Connecticut River valley area of Massachusetts. In addition, sources of irrigation have been dwindling. Many communities in central and eastern Massachusetts have imposed mandatory non-essential outdoor water use restrictions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor expanded the Severe Drought (D2) designation into western Hampshire County on August 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed and moved across southern New England ahead of a cold front beginning after midnight. These storms were mainly characterized by heavy rain and flooding, but one storm produced a rare early morning tornado in Concord, MA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed within an area of showers and thunderstorms moving through southern New England.  This tornado touched down near the Cambridge Turnpike and headed northeast.  Most of the damage was concentrated in an area beginning near the intersection of Lexington Road and Alcott Road and continuing up to the neighborhood of Alcott and Independence Roads.  Numerous trees were uprooted or had the tops sheared off.  These subsequently blocked roads, damaged homes, and downed power lines, cutting off power to the neighborhood.  In addition, utility poles were downed either from the wind or from the downed power lines.  Thirty-nine houses in this area were damaged to some degree.  Only one house suffered significant structural damage.  The tornado continued for a short distance beyond this neighborhood before lifting.  The historical home of Louisa May Alcott and her family was right next to the tornado path but was not damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved from the eastern Great Lakes towards the Northeast coast on Thursday, October 27th. With plenty of unseasonably cold air in place, steady precipitation in the form of the snow moved across the region ahead of an advancing warm front during the morning and afternoon hours. ||Precipitation was fairly light and snow only accumulated up to four inches, with the highest amounts across the hills and mountains. Despite these minor amounts, travel was largely disrupted across the region as a result of the snowfall with some traffic accidents occurring. ||Precipitation tapered off to rain and snow showers during the late evening hours, as the storm system exited off to the east.

Mid level energy resulted in showers and a few isolated thunderstorms moving east across southern New England from New York during the early morning hours.  Brief heavy downpours with these showers resulted in a few areas of minor flooding, particularly in West Springfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Sylvan and Park Streets was flooded and impassable.  The Route 5 tunnel was flooded and traffic was rerouted to the rotary above the tunnel.  The Union Street underpass was flooded and impassable with damage to the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thorndike Street or Route 32 was flooded both at the underpass with Interstate 90 and near the Big Y supermarket parking lot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: August brought more dry weather and continued drought conditions to much of southern New England.  By the end of the month, most, if not all of Massachusetts was in a drought designation.  For many, the drought began in July 2016.  At this point, this is a drought categorized largely by well below normal precipitation and groundwater and agricultural impacts.  Most of the region experienced below normal rainfall during the month of August.  Much of eastern Massachusetts south of Boston, received less than half the normal amount of rainfall for the month.  The exception was southern Worcester County where above normal rainfall was experienced.  However, this additional rainfall did little to dispel drought conditions.  Boston experienced its driest meteorological summer (June/July/August) on record with only 3.92 inches of rainfall.  The previous record was 3.97 inches in 1957.  ||The Massachusetts Executive Office of Energy and Environmental Affairs issued a drought warning for the northeast and central portions of the state, a drought watch for southeast Massachusetts and the Connecticut River Valley of Massachusetts, and a drought advisory for western Massachusetts and Cape Cod and the Islands on August 12th. This was an upgrade for each region from July.  Of note: this is the first time a Drought Warning has been in effect for any portion of Massachusetts since the original creation of the state's drought plan in 2001.  Soil moisture (measured by the Climate Prediction Center) was abnormally dry, except across east coastal Massachusetts where conditions were found to be excessively dry.  Groundwater conditions (measured by the United States Geological Survey) were found to be below to well below normal for most of Massachusetts. Cape Cod and the Islands were at or above normal at this time. River and streamflow conditions were below to well below normal with 27 gage sites in southern New England at record low levels.||Farmers have had to irrigate their crops much more than normal for August.  Losses were started to be reported by producers in the Connecticut River valley area of Massachusetts.  In addition, sources of irrigation have been dwindling.  While data thus far has been anecdotal, many farmers are having to choose which fields to irrigate and which to let go, as well as not doing 2nd and 3rd plantings of late summer crops.  Known losses, according to the Massachusetts Farm Bureau Federation, include a total loss of the peach crop, reduced apple and cranberry crops, and no second and third cut hay.  More than 160 public water systems in Massachusetts have imposed some sort of water use restrictions, 70 of those have the strictest parameters in place, allowing residents to use outdoor water one day a week or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor expanded the Severe Drought (D2) designation into eastern Franklin County on August 2nd.

The exception was southern Worcester County where above normal rainfall was experienced.  However, this additional rainfall did little to dispel drought conditions.  Boston experienced its driest meteorological summer (June/July/August) on record with only 3.92 inches of rainfall.  The previous record was 3.97 inches in 1957.  ||The Massachusetts Executive Office of Energy and Environmental Affairs issued a drought warning for the northeast and central portions of the state, a drought watch for southeast Massachusetts and the Connecticut River Valley of Massachusetts, and a drought advisory for western Massachusetts and Cape Cod and the Islands on August 12th. This was an upgrade for each region from July.  Of note: this is the first time a Drought Warning has been in effect for any portion of Massachusetts since the original creation of the state's drought plan in 2001.  Soil moisture (measured by the Climate Prediction Center) was abnormally dry, except across east coastal Massachusetts where conditions were found to be excessively dry.  Groundwater conditions (measured by the United States Geological Survey) were found to be below to well below normal for most of Massachusetts. Cape Cod and the Islands were at or above normal at this time. River and streamflow conditions were below to well below normal with 27 gage sites in southern New England at record low levels.||Farmers have had to irrigate their crops much more than normal for August.  Losses were started to be reported by producers in the Connecticut River valley area of Massachusetts.  In addition, sources of irrigation have been dwindling.  While data thus far has been anecdotal, many farmers are having to choose which fields to irrigate and which to let go, as well as not doing 2nd and 3rd plantings of late summer crops.  Known losses, according to the Massachusetts Farm Bureau Federation, include a total loss of the peach crop, reduced apple and cranberry crops, and no second and third cut hay.  More than 160 public water systems in Massachusetts have imposed some sort of water use restrictions, 70 of those have the strictest parameters in place, allowing residents to use outdoor water one day a week or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor continued the Severe Drought (D2) designation in western Essex County through August 16th.  It was upgraded to an Extreme Drought (D3) designation at that time, which continued through the end of August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed south and east of southern New England bringing rain to much of the area south of the Massachusetts Turnpike.  Most of this rain was gentle and much needed, however, Nantucket Island received three to four inches of rain across the island with much of it falling within a couple of hours.  This resulted in flooding on Nantucket.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One of the control tower operators at Nantucket Memorial Airport reported flooding in the parking lot of the airport, with cars stalled due to the depth of the water.  Amateur radio operators reported eight inches of water flooding Sparks Avenue.  A report on social media indicated significant flooding in the main airport loop, but no stalled cars a couple of hours after the report from the control tower operator.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance moved through southern New England resulting in scattered showers and thunderstorms.  One of these storms produced heavy rainfall that resulted in flooding in Shrewsbury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 9 between Crescent Street and Maple Avenue was impassable due to flooding.  Five cars were stuck in the flood waters over this stretch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was above normal in October for Eastern Massachusetts and the Central Hills, but remained below normal in Western Massachusetts.  Values since July 2016 for all areas remained below normal. ||On October 7th, the Massachusetts Executive Office of Energy and Environmental Affairs expanded the Drought Warning to include the Connecticut River region. A Drought Warning remained in effect for the northeast, central, and southeast parts of the state. A Drought Watch continued for Cape Cod and parts of the Islands.  The Massachusetts Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) indicated mandatory non-essential outdoor water use restrictions were in place for numerous communities within central and eastern MA. Other communities in the Commonwealth had voluntary water restrictions in place.||Many gages in central and eastern portions of southern New England had at least somewhat improved streamflows. However, across the Connecticut River Valley in MA and CT, rivers and streams remained quite low.||At the end of October, many USGS groundwater wells across southern New England were at below to well below normal levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor placed northwest Middlesex County in an Extreme Drought (D3) designation through the month of October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning started a fire on Joint Base Cape Cod on July 22nd.  The fire was discovered early Saturday (July 23) but burned through the night and into the following day (July 24).  The fire was contained to 125 acres after 36 hours of fighting the fire.  Dry conditions throughout southern New England contributed to the spread of the fire through dry brush and trees.  Four helicopters from the Massachusetts State Police and the Army National Guard shuttled back and forth between Snake Pond in Sandwich and the areas on the base that were inaccessible to firefighters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service first learned of this fire via the Massachusetts State Police social media feeds, then later received additional information via the Cape Cod Times and Massachusetts Emergency Management Agency.  The fire was reported to be contained to unused, wildland portions of Joint Base Cape Cod.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and unstable air mass was in place over western Massachusetts ahead of an advancing cold front on Monday, July 18th. Short lines of thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon hours and moved eastward across the region. ||Some of these thunderstorms became severe, producing damaging wind gusts to trees and power lines. The most significant damage occurred in the town of Cheshire, where power was knocked out to around 1300 customers.  Most of the power outages were restored by the evening hours.||Thunderstorms ended with the passage of the cold front during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed and blocking a road on Main Road in Savoy due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a departing cold front, a strong pressure gradient in place allowed for gusty winds across the entire region between the evening on Saturday, October 22nd and through the entire day on Sunday, October 23rd. West to northwest winds gusted up to 50 MPH at times, resulting in downed trees and power lines over parts of western Massachusetts. Some isolated power outages occurred as a result of the downed wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with a warm, humid, unstable environment to produce showers and thunderstorms across southern New England. Much of this activity was focused in and around Rhode Island. Plenty of moisture in the atmosphere resulted in heavy rain with these storms and some flooding was reported in addition to damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto wires, blocking Elm Street between Taft Street and Cider Mill Lane in Upton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through southern New England during the peak heating of the day, taking full advantage of the available instability. This resulted in showers and thunderstorms that produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surveys done by emergency management, fire, and police officials as well as NWS storm spotters and discussed with the NWS office determined that straight line winds with speeds up to 80 mph caused the damage that occurred in Haverhill.  Numerous trees and wires were downed throughout Haverhill with some blocking streets and others falling onto houses and cars.  This included at least 50 trees that fell at the Haverhill Country Club.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires on Morton Street and Bay Road were downed onto wires by thunderstorms.  One of the trees struck a house on Bay Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking toward southern New England tapped into a plume of tropical moisture and produced torrential rainfall over parts of central and northeast Massachusetts in the matter of a few hours. This resulted in prolific urban flash flooding in the greater Worcester area and significant urban flooding in greater Boston. The highest single rainfall total from this event was within the City of Worcester, where a Skywarn Spotter reported 5.24 inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of rain fell in the matter of a few hours on the evening of October 21 and resulted in significant urban flash flooding in Wayland. Many roads were flooded and impassable including parts of Routes 20, 27, 30, and 126, East Plain and School Streets, and Windy Hill Lane near Country Club and Storm Bridge Road. Old Connecticut Path near Wayland High School was flooded with water reaching the tops of car tires. A car was stuck in flood waters at the intersection of Pequot Road and Happy Hollow Road. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Matthew was a major hurricane in the Caribbean and Bahamas, then weakened along the Southeast US coast and became post-tropical off North Carolina. The remnants of Matthew merged with a cold front off the coast and moved past Cape Cod during the night of October 9th-10th. This caused wind damage in parts of Eastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS site at Nantucket Airport reported a sustained south wind of 46 mph and a gust of 58 mph at 655 pm EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving from the Great Lakes to Cape Cod brought strong winds to coastal Rhode Island and strong to high winds to coastal Massachusetts.  The high winds were from the southeast during the overnight and early morning hours as the storm approached and from the northwest in the afternoon as the storm departed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operators reported a wind gust to 58 mph in East Falmouth at 2:56 AM EDT.  A Weatherflow wind site at West Dennis recorded sustained high winds of 43 mph at 440 AM EDT and a Weatherflow site at Wellfleet recorded sustained high winds of 44 mph at 550 AM EDT.  Later in the afternoon, on the northwest winds, a Weatherflow site measured sustained winds of 45 mph at Hatch Beach in Eastham at 613 PM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Maritimes brought cold air to interior parts of Southern New England. Low pressure moved from the Great Lakes to the Massachusetts coast bringing  several inches of wet snow to the interior.  Most trees still had leaves at this time, allowing the snow to cause a significant amount of tree damage. The damage was most pronounced in the Connecticut River Valley in western Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few inches of wet snow fell in the western part of southern Worcester County. Because it was early in the season and most trees still had leaves, there was some damage.  Amateur radio operators reported trees down on wires at Rondeau Street in Warren.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across New England Thursday afternoon and evening followed by strong northwest winds and cold air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds brought a tree down on Cohannet Street in Taunton blocking the road. Winds brought a tree down on Foundry Street in Easton, blocking one lane of the road. Two trees were brought down on Smith Street in Attleboro. The wind at Taunton Airport gusted to 45 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was brought down on wires on Masterson Road in Whately and blocked the road.  A tree and wires were brought down on North Main Street in New Salem.  Winds at Orange gusted to 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across New England Thursday afternoon and evening followed by strong northwest winds and cold air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds at Worcester Airport gusted to 54 mph. The sustained wind reached 39 mph. A tree was brought down on a house on Quaker Street in Northbridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across New England Thursday afternoon and evening followed by strong northwest winds and cold air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 49 mph at the Westfield-Barnes Regional Airport. Sustained winds reached 36 mph. Wires were brought down on Maple Road in Longmeadow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from Long Island across southeastern Massachusetts on the 29th and then rapidly deepened as it moved northeastward into the Gulf of Maine overnight on the 30th. Precipitation was mainly rain along the Boston-Providence corridor. However 3 to 6 inches of snow was common in western and central Massachusetts, with 5 to 7 inches in the slopes of the Berkshires and up to 8 to 11 inches in the higher terrain of northern Worcester County. ||As the storm intensified, thundersnow occurred in Boston on the evening of the 29th. Strong winds occurred behind the storm during the overnight hours, causing scattered tree and power line damage, with gusts mainly in the 30-50 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 5.5 to 7.0 inches in western Franklin County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak wave of low pressure developed near the southern New England coast on the 11th and moved northeastward.  It brought mainly light to moderate snow across the region, before changing to rain and ending late in the morning on the 12th.  However, in the slopes of the Berkshires, in the higher terrain of northwest Hampshire and western Franklin Counties in Massachusetts, up to 6 to 7 inches of snow was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of 5.5 to 6.0 inches in western Franklin County, including Buckland, Ashfield, Heath, Rowe, and Colrain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak wave of low pressure developed near the southern New England coast on the 11th and moved northeastward.  It brought mainly light to moderate snow across the region, before changing to rain and ending late in the morning on the 12th.  However, in the slopes of the Berkshires, in the higher terrain of northwest Hampshire and western Franklin Counties in Massachusetts, up to 6 to 7 inches of snow was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 6 to 7 inches were reported in Plainfield, in the northwest corner of Hampshire County near the Franklin County line.  A report of 6.9 inches was received via amateur radio and 6.1 inches by a CoCoRAHS observer.  Amounts were closer to 4 to 5 inches in southwestern Hampshire County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of rain fell within the matter of a few hours during the evening of October 21 which resulted in prolific urban flash flooding in Worcester and surrounding areas. ||In Worcester, flash flooding made roads impassable and there were many reports of cars stuck in floodwaters and people needing rescue. Roads flooded included Cambridge, Chandler, Bancroft, Park, Harding, Hammond, Highland, Summer, Lincoln, Green, Grove, Bridge, Irving and Ellsworth Streets, as well as Goldthwaite Road, Pleasant Terrace, Major Taylor Boulevard, Shore Drive, and Park Avenue. Several vehicles were stuck in floodwaters on I-290.||In Fitchburg, flash flooding closed a section of the John Fitchburg Highway and a car was stuck in flood waters on Shelley Avenue.  In Leominster, flash flooding closed the intersection of Washington Street and Grove Avenue as well as McKay Street entering the hospital. In Lancaster, Main Street was flooded and impassable while in Shrewsbury, Route 9 at Oak Street was flooded up to the tops of car tires.||No injuries were reported due to the flash flooding.||Due to the ongoing drought conditions, stream and river flows were well below average and the excessive amount of rainfall did not produce flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over northern New England, combined with a strong upper level disturbance sweeping across New England, ushered in colder air and strong to damaging wind gusts across across Massachusetts and Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several scattered reports of trees downed in northern Worcester County. Single trees were reported down in: Rutland, Barre, Leicester, and Oakham (on Spencer Road).  Two trees were downed in Worcester (on Coldbrook Road and South Road). The Worcester Airport reported sustained winds of 35 mph and a gust to 48 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Maritimes brought cold air to interior parts of Southern New England. Low pressure moved from the Great Lakes to the Massachusetts coast bringing  several inches of wet snow to the interior.  Most trees still had leaves at this time, allowing the snow to cause a significant amount of tree damage. The damage was most pronounced in the Connecticut River Valley in western Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An average of two to five inches of snow fell in eastern Hampshire County. However, because it was early in the season and most trees still had leaves, there was quite a bit of damage.  There were many reports from the heavily populated city of Northampton. In Northampton, a tree was down on a house on Pentcastle Avenue; trees and wires were down at Fort Hill Terrace, Clement Street, Hockanum Road; wires were down on Elm Street, Hatfield Street, and Adare Place; a tree was down on wires at Mount Tom, Loudville Road at Westhampton Road, Mountain Road, Old Ferry Road, and North Farms Road; and a tree was blocking the road at the intersection of Depot and Main Streets.  Elsewhere in eastern Hampshire County, a tree was down on wires at the intersection of Pine and Pleasant Streets in Amherst.  In Hadley, amateur radio operators reported a tree down on a car at North Maple Street.  Also in Hadley, a tree and power lines were down at Breckenridge Road.  Wires were down on Gore Avenue in Hatfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure rapidly developed off the coast of the mid-Atlantic and moved northeast towards southern New England on Thursday, December 29th. Steady snowfall developed during the morning hours and a light to moderate snowfall occurred throughout the entire day. The snowfall tapered off from west to east during the evening hours. Most areas picked up 3 to 7 inches of snowfall, with the highest amounts across the higher elevations. The snowfall allowed for slow travel on area roadways and some minor traffic accidents as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Maritimes brought cold air to interior parts of Southern New England. Low pressure moved from the Great Lakes to the Massachusetts coast bringing  several inches of wet snow to the interior.  Most trees still had leaves at this time, allowing the snow to cause a significant amount of tree damage. The damage was most pronounced in the Connecticut River Valley in western Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An average of one to four inches of wet snow fell in eastern Franklin County. However, because it was early in the season and most trees still had leaves, there was some damage.  Amateur radio operators reported downed wires and a transformer, blocking Long Plain Road in Whately.  Trees and wires were downed on Country Club Road and on Madison Circle in Greenfield and a tree was also downed on Laurel Street in Greenfield. Trees and wires were downed on Jennison Road in Wendell.  A tree was down on wires on West Pelham Road in Shutesbury.  Wires were reported down on Meadow Road in Montague.  In New Salem, a tree fell on wires and blocked a portion of Route 202.  Multiple trees and wires were reported downed in Gill on Pisgah Mountain Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking toward southern New England tapped into a plume of tropical moisture and produced torrential rainfall over parts of central and northeast Massachusetts in the matter of a few hours. This resulted in prolific urban flash flooding in the greater Worcester area and significant urban flooding in greater Boston. The highest single rainfall total from this event was within the City of Worcester, where a Skywarn Spotter reported 5.24 inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of rain in a short time produced significant urban flash flooding in and around Boston. Two people were rescued from a car stuck in two feet of flood waters on Malcolm X Boulevard in Boston, while flooding also closed a portion of Columbia Road near I-93. Flooding also affected the Alewife T Station. In Chelsea, flooding was reported on Everett Avenue from the high school and on Beech Street. In Mattapan, a section of Harvard Street was flooded between Walk Hill and Morton Streets.

This resulted in wind chill values as low as 30 degrees below zero on late Thursday night into early Friday morning. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a cold air mass in place over all of western Massachusetts, a weak warm front approached from the southwest during the early morning hours on Saturday, December 17th. At same time, another weak wave of low pressure developed along the mid-Atlantic coast and lifted northeast towards New England. Steady snow developed during the early morning hours and continued until about the early afternoon before changing over to a wintry mix or snow, sleet and freezing rain. Eventually, precipitation tapered off by the late afternoon hours. By that time, about 3 to 8 inches of snow accumulated across Berkshire County.||The snowfall resulted in slippery roads and traffic delays, but no major issues were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: July brought the start of drought conditions in parts of southern New England.  Much of the water year was wrought with below normal precipitation for much, if not all of the region.  December of 2015 and February of 2016 saw normal to slightly above normal numbers but this was not enough to overcome the deficits in the other 10 months.  Taking the year as a whole (October 2015-September 2016) all of southern New England is at least ten to fifteen inches below normal for precipitation.  The one exception is Nantucket, which is less than five inches below normal.  Most of the area is at least fifteen inches below normal for the water year.  ||Despite the precipitation deficits, the U.S. Drought Monitor (a collaboration between the University of Nebraska-Lincoln, the U.S. Department of Agriculture, and the National Weather Service) did not declare a severe drought in southern New England until July 2016.||The Massachusetts Executive Office of Energy and Enviornmental Affairs issued a drought watch for the northeast and central portions of the state and a drought advisory for southeast Massachusetts and the Connecticut River Valley of Massachusetts on July 8th.  Soil moisture (measured by the Climate Prediction Center) and groundwater conditions (measured by the United States Geological Survey) were found to be abnormally dry or much below normal for most of Massachusetts.  Cape Cod and the Islands were at or above normal at this time.  River and streamflow conditions were below to well below normal July 8th and seventeen gage sites were at record low levels.||While no direct agricultural impacts were reported for the month of July, farmers were having to irrigate their crops much more than is normal for July.||An unusual result of the dry conditions that persisted through the first half of the year was the thriving gypsy moth population.  These caterpillars are usually killed by a fungus earlier in the year, but with a lack of rain and moisture, these caterpillars thrived and then decimated tree foliage in much of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor expanded the Severe Drought (D2) designation into western Middlesex County on July 5th.
