EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moving through southern New England produced strong northwesterly winds, mainly during the afternoon and early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds gusting to 45 to 50 mph causing wires to be blown down on Tercentennial Drive and a large branch down on Linnell Circle in Billerica.  In Sudbury, a tree and wires were down on Hudson Road and Lancaster Road.  Wind gusts of 44 mph at Worcester Airport ASOS and 49 mph at a Cambridge amateur radio station were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moving through southern New England produced strong northwesterly winds, mainly during the afternoon and early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusting to about 45 mph knocked down a tree on River Street, causing power outages.  Both the Fitchburg and Worcester ASOS stations reported wind gusts to 44 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was below normal for all of Massachusetts during November, with amounts in Southeast Massachusetts ranging from near 2 to 3 inches below normal.||A Drought Warning remains in effect for most of Massachusetts.  A Drought Advisory continued for Cape Cod and the Islands. ||November average streamflows were mostly below or significantly below normal in every drought region. Groundwater levels were below normal in all areas except in the Northeast and Cape/Islands. Reservoir levels were below normal for all regions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor continued the Extreme Drought (D3) designation for northwest Middlesex County through the month of November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front combined with high instability and moisture to produce showers and thunderstorms across much of southern New England.  Some of these storms became strong enough to produce wind damage, while heavy rain resulted in some minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree on Turners Falls Road was downed onto wires by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary stalled across the I-95 corridor and was the focus for showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon. A few of these storms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Weather Observation System at Provincetown Municipal Airport recorded a wind gust of 63 mph associated with a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through southern New England and when coupled with the existing heat and humidity, resulted in showers and thunderstorms developing over much of the area late in the day and continuing into the evening and overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees along Route 9 were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through southern New England and when coupled with the existing heat and humidity, resulted in showers and thunderstorms developing over much of the area late in the day and continuing into the evening and overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires on Rocky Hill Road and Farm Lane were downed by thunderstorm winds.  One of these trees closed Farm Lane until the tree could be removed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through southern New England and when coupled with the existing heat and humidity, resulted in showers and thunderstorms developing over much of the area late in the day and continuing into the evening and overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house on Main Road in Westport, starting a fire.  The fire affected the second floor and attic of the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through southern New England and when coupled with the existing heat and humidity, resulted in showers and thunderstorms developing over much of the area late in the day and continuing into the evening and overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A concentrated area of wind damage in Westborough was found by an NWS storm survey team around Chauncy and Oak Streets and extended ENE to the east shore of Chauncy Pond on the old Westborough State Hospital property and to the ESE along Flanders Street.  Numerous hardwood and softwood trees, some very large, were uprooted or snapped off.  A few landed on houses and vehicles.  Most of the downed trees were lying in an easterly direction except more toward the ENE along the east shore of Chauncy Pond, consistent with what one might expect to be something of a divergent pattern with a microburst.  At the southern edge of the damage swath no more than a quarter mile from downtown Westborough, the trees and branches seemed to be lying in a more ESE direction, again suggesting a hint of a divergent pattern.  Several signs were blown down along Route 9, including a large restaurant sign and a billboard.  A resident along Chauncy Street who took video looking out of his basement window showed an image that showed the storm approaching at 7:55 PM EDT and damage occurring at 8:02 PM EDT.  The concentrated part of the microburst began about a half mile west of the Route 9 and Lyman Street intersection and ended about three quarters of a mile east of that point.  The westernmost sign of damage observed was a tree down along Otis Street.  It is possible that some damage occurred further west but was not part of the survey area.  The team encountered one of the largest root balls they had ever seen on the old Westborough State Hospital grounds.  Scattered damage was observed to the east into Southborough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through southern New England during the peak heating of the day, taking full advantage of the available instability. This resulted in showers and thunderstorms that produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree on Route 213 in Methuen was downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold pool aloft coupled with a large area of instability and a mid level disturbance moving through the area resulted in showers and thunderstorms across much of southern New England.  The stronger of these storms produced hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through southern New England and when coupled with the existing heat and humidity, resulted in showers and thunderstorms developing over much of the area late in the day and continuing into the evening and overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to damage in Westborough, the NWS storm survey team found another concentrated area of damage in Southborough in the vicinity of Central Street, just north of Route 9.  In particular, concentrated damage was observed along Winter Street off Central Street and to the north along Route 30/Boston Road, where a series of telephone poles were snapped off.  The dimensions of this area were about a quarter of a mile square.  A large Norway spruce tree was uprooted onto a house on Winter Street and other trees completely blocked Winter Street for a time.  A gazebo was crushed by a large tree at a park off Central and Winter Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure approached western Massachusetts from the Great Lakes during the late evening hours on Sunday, December 11th. Light snow eventually broke out around the region and continued into the overnight hours. The steadiest snowfall occurred just prior to daybreak on Monday, December 12th, with rates around one half inch per hour. ||As the storm system lifted northwest of the region, steady snowfall tapered off during the mid-morning hours on Monday, December 12th. By that time, about 3 to 6 inches of snow fell over much of the area.

An area of low pressure approached western Massachusetts from the Great Lakes during the late evening hours on Sunday, December 11th. Light snow eventually broke out around the region and continued into the overnight hours. The steadiest snowfall occurred just prior to daybreak on Monday, December 12th, with rates around one half inch per hour. ||As the storm system lifted northwest of the region, steady snowfall tapered off during the mid-morning hours on Monday, December 12th. By that time, about 3 to 6 inches of snow fell over much of the area.

Low pressure moved from Long Island across southeastern Massachusetts on the 29th and then rapidly deepened as it moved northeastward into the Gulf of Maine overnight on the 30th. Precipitation was mainly rain along the Boston-Providence corridor. However 3 to 6 inches of snow was common in western and central Massachusetts, with 5 to 7 inches in the slopes of the Berkshires and up to 8 to 11 inches in the higher terrain of northern Worcester County. ||As the storm intensified, thundersnow occurred in Boston on the evening of the 29th. Strong winds occurred behind the storm during the overnight hours, causing scattered tree and power line damage, with gusts mainly in the 30-50 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds behind the storm caused some damage. In Rutland, a tree was down on Glenwood Road. In Barre, a tree was reported down on Granger Road. Wind gusts were estimated to be only 30-35 mph, but it may be partially due to the weight of the freshly fallen snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area tracked across northeast MA late on Nov. 20 and moved northeast of the region on Nov. 21. A potent upper level disturbance moved across southern New England on the 20th.  ||Rain changed to a period of heavy wet snow across the higher terrain above 1000 feet in the slopes of the Berkshires early in the morning on the 20th.  Snow accumulated 3 to 5 inches by 8 AM on the 20th.  Then another 2 to 4 inches occurred from the 20th into early on the 21st...with 2-day totals reaching 9 inches in Heath and 6 inches in Rowe, both in Franklin County, MA.  This did not reach Winter Storm Warning temporal thresholds.  ||Gusty southwest winds also occurred on the 20th, followed by strong west winds with a few pockets of high winds behind the system on the 21st, causing some damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 40-50 mph knocked down several large tree limbs in Heath during the late afternoon on the 21st.

Groundwater levels were below normal in all areas except in the Northeast and Cape/Islands. Reservoir levels were below normal for all regions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On November 1, the U.S. Drought Monitor reduced the designation for eastern Plymouth County to Moderate Drought (D1).  This continued through the end of the month.

Groundwater levels were below normal in all areas except in the Northeast and Cape/Islands. Reservoir levels were below normal for all regions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On November 1, the U.S. Drought Monitor reduced the designation for southern Plymouth County to Moderate Drought (D1).  This continued through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold pool aloft coupled with a large area of instability and a mid level disturbance moving through the area resulted in showers and thunderstorms across much of southern New England.  The stronger of these storms produced hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed by thunderstorm winds throughout town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold pool aloft coupled with a large area of instability and a mid level disturbance moving through the area resulted in showers and thunderstorms across much of southern New England.  The stronger of these storms produced hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large branch was downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold pool aloft coupled with a large area of instability and a mid level disturbance moving through the area resulted in showers and thunderstorms across much of southern New England.  The stronger of these storms produced hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree on Willow Street was downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation was below normal for most of Massachusetts during December, but with areas of above normal amounts along the North Shore and around Quabbin Reservoir in Central Massachusetts.  Amounts in Southeast Massachusetts were near 2 inches below normal while Amherst was near 1 inch above normal.||A Drought Warning remains in effect for most of Massachusetts. A Drought Advisory continued for Cape Cod and the Islands. ||Although rivers and streams had some fluctuations during the month, they were predominantly at below normal levels during December. Reservoirs monitored by Massachusetts Department of Conservation and Recreation (DCR), were mainly at below normal levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor continued the Extreme Drought (D3) designation for western Middlesex County through the month of December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed south of New England Friday night the 16th, moved along the Southern New England coast on Saturday the 17th, and moved to the Maritimes by Sunday the 18th.  This combined with cold air already in place over the region brought a broad area of snow over Southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven to eight inches of snow fell on western Franklin County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across New England Thursday afternoon and evening followed by strong northwest winds and cold air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 58 mph at Blue Hill in Milton.  Sustained winds at Blue Hill reached 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across New England Thursday afternoon and evening followed by strong northwest winds and cold air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 63 mph in Nantucket.  Winds at Nantucket Airport gusted to 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of Saturday, August 13th, a warm front lifted northward across the region. This allowed a very warm and excessively humid air mass to be in place over the area. ||Late in the day, a surface cold front was approaching the area from the west. An organized line of thunderstorms moved from west to east across all of eastern Upstate New York and crossed western Massachusetts during the evening hours. These storms produced gusty winds, which caused damage to some trees and power lines. ||In addition to the gusty winds, the thunderstorms also produced a significant amount of cloud-to-ground lightning. Numerous power outages were reported across the region as a result of the thunderstorms. ||Thunderstorms ended with the passage of the cold front during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A telephone pole and wires were downed on Bellevue Avenue in North Adams due to thunderstorm winds.
